range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo
situation: Conservative ranges. First to act.
question: You have A♠A♣ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: AA raises from every position. 3 BB is the standard sizing for every hand. Hardin's five reasons to raise: show strength, gain initiative, thin the field, build the pot, steal the blinds. Uniform sizing means opponents cannot read your hand strength.

why_wrong.1: Limping AA signals weakness with a capped range. You surrender fold equity and give everyone a cheap flop.
why_wrong.2: Never fold AA preflop.
why_wrong.3: Raising larger with strong hands telegraphs strength. Always 3 BB regardless of hand strength.

range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo
situation: Conservative ranges. First to act.
question: You have 6♣6♦ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: 66 is the bottom of the conservative UTG range (66+). It qualifies. Raise 3 BB. Hardin notes: smaller pairs 22-55 often end up check/folding OOP on bad flops, which is why the UTG threshold starts at 66.

why_wrong.1: 66 is in the conservative UTG range. Fold is too tight.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act — raise or fold only.
why_wrong.3: Always 3 BB — uniform sizing for all hands.

range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo
situation: Conservative ranges. First to act.
question: You have 5♠5♦ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: The conservative UTG range starts at 66+. 55 does not qualify. Five players still to act means high risk of running into a better hand, and 55 often ends up check/folding OOP. Fold. (55 is fine from MP onwards.)

why_wrong.1: 55 is below the 66+ conservative UTG threshold.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act.
why_wrong.3: Smaller sizing does not change that 55 is below threshold.

range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo
situation: Conservative ranges. First to act.
question: You have J♠T♠ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: JTs is explicitly listed in Hardin's conservative UTG range (66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo). It is one of the very few suited connectors allowed from UTG. Raise 3 BB.

why_wrong.1: JTs is in the UTG range. Fold is too tight.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act.
why_wrong.3: JTs is a legitimate UTG open — late position is not required.

range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo
situation: Conservative ranges. First to act.
question: You have T♣9♣ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: T9s is NOT in the conservative UTG range. Only JTs is listed as a suited connector in conservative UTG. T9s appears in the moderate UTG range. Fold from UTG conservative.

why_wrong.1: T9s is below the conservative UTG threshold. Only JTs qualifies as a suited connector from UTG conservative.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act.
why_wrong.3: Non-standard sizing does not fix a hand below threshold.

range_context: Conservative UTG (9.80%): 66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo

question: You have K♦J♠ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: KJo is NOT in the conservative UTG range. The minimum off-suit king is KQo. KJo faces domination from KQ, AK, and AJ — exactly the kinds of hands in UTG's calling range. Fold.

why_wrong.1: KJo UTG faces reverse implied odds. You flop a king and lose big to KQ or AK.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act.
why_wrong.3: Smaller sizing does not fix a dominated hand.

explanation: In the moderate UTG range (12.07%), the pocket pair threshold drops to 55+. 55 qualifies. Raise 3 BB. In conservative UTG, 55 does not qualify — this is the key difference.

why_wrong.1: 55 is in the moderate UTG range. Fold is too tight.
why_wrong.2: Never limp as first to act.
why_wrong.3: Always 3 BB.

question: You have Q♠Q♦ in UTG. You are first to act. What do you do?
explanation: QQ is in the conservative UTG range (66+). Always raise. Note: QQ will be a value 3-bet facing a re-raise, but as first action it is always a standard open raise of 3 BB.

why_wrong.1: Limping QQ UTG is a fundamental mistake — you surrender fold equity and initiative.
why_wrong.2: Never fold QQ preflop.
why_wrong.3: Uniform 3 BB — never telegraph hand strength with larger sizing.

why_wrong.1: ATs is in the UTG range. Fold is too tight.

explanation: AKo is in the conservative UTG range (AJo+ includes AJo, AQo, AKo, and all suited versions). Raise 3 BB — same as any other hand. No special sizing for premium hands.

why_wrong.1: AKo does not warrant a larger sizing. Uniform 3 BB for all hands — mixing up sizing telegraphs strength.
why_wrong.2: Never limp AKo as first action. You surrender the initiative and build no pot.
why_wrong.3: AKo is always a raise from UTG.

range_context: UTG raise -> BB 3-bets. UTG 4-bet range: AA, KK, AKs. Fold: TT and below, AQo and below.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. BB 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have A♠A♦ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, BB 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: AA always 4-bets facing a 3-bet. OOP 4-bet sizing: 2-2.2x the 3-bet = ~22 BB. You want calls from KK, QQ, AK. Never slow-play AA facing a 3-bet.

why_wrong.1: Calling AA OOP vs a 3-bet misses value — you have the best hand, build the pot.
why_wrong.2: Never fold AA.
why_wrong.3: Shoving 100 BB folds out QQ, JJ which you want calls from.

range_context: UTG raise -> CO 3-bets. UTG 4-bet range: AA, KK, AKs. Fold: TT and below, AQo and below.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. CO 3-bets to 9 BB.
question: You have K♣K♦ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, CO 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: KK always 4-bets facing a 3-bet. IP 4-bet sizing: 2-2.2x the 3-bet = ~20 BB. You are still OOP vs CO postflop so build the pot now.

why_wrong.1: Calling KK OOP in a 3-bet pot is second-best — build the pot with the second best hand.
why_wrong.2: Never fold KK facing a 3-bet.
why_wrong.3: Shoving 100 BB folds out QQ, JJ, AK which you want calls from.

range_context: UTG raise -> BTN 3-bets. UTG 4-bet range: AA, KK, AKs. AKo also 4-bets here.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. BTN 3-bets to 9 BB.
question: You have A♥K♣ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, BTN 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: AKo 4-bets vs a BTN 3-bet. BTN 3-bets a wide polarized range from position — AKo has strong equity vs all bluffs and most value hands. Hardin's data: 3-betting AK yields 797 BB/100 vs 330 BB/100 when calling. 4-bet to ~20 BB (2.2x the 3-bet).

why_wrong.1: Calling AKo OOP vs BTN's polarized 3-bet is 2.4x less profitable than 4-betting.
why_wrong.2: AKo is ahead of BTN's 3-bet range except AA/KK. Fold is wrong.
why_wrong.3: Shoving folds out JJ, QQ which you want calls from.

range_context: UTG raise -> MP 3-bets. MP 3-bet range vs UTG is very tight: AA-QQ, AKs, optional JJ/AKo. TT is below this.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. MP 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have T♠T♦ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, MP 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: TT vs MP 3-bet OOP = fold. MP's 3-bet range vs UTG is extremely tight — QQ+, AKs at minimum. TT is behind QQ+ and flipping with AKs. OOP at 100 BB, fold is correct. If stacks were 150 BB+ you could consider calling for set-mining equity.

why_wrong.1: Calling TT OOP in a 3-bet pot vs MP's tight range leads to expensive spots on every overcard board.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting TT into QQ+/AKs is suicidal — you're almost always behind.
why_wrong.3: Shoving TT is a massive overplay.

range_context: UTG raise -> SB 3-bets. SB 3-bet range vs UTG: AA-TT, AJ+. JJ is a fold from UTG OOP.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. SB 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have J♣J♦ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, SB 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: JJ vs SB 3-bet from UTG = fold. SB's default 3-bet range vs any position includes AA-TT, AJ+. JJ is behind QQ, KK, AA and flipping with AK. OOP at 100 BB, fold. The raise/fold line: raise preflop, fold to a tight 3-bet.

why_wrong.1: Calling JJ OOP in a 3-bet pot vs SB's tight range puts you in difficult spots on every A/K/Q board.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting JJ into QQ+/AK is usually behind.
why_wrong.3: Shoving JJ is a massive overplay.

range_context: UTG raise -> BB 3-bets. QQ vs UTG is optional 4-bet (default = AA/KK/AKs, optional = QQ/AKo).
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. BB 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have Q♠Q♥ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, BB 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: QQ is listed as optional in the UTG 4-bet range (default = AA/KK/AKs). Despite being optional, QQ has clear equity advantage vs BB's 3-bet range and should 4-bet. OOP sizing 2-2.2x = ~22 BB. If BB 5-bets jam, you face a tough decision vs a very tight player.

why_wrong.1: Calling QQ OOP in a 3-bet pot is second-best — you have equity dominance over most of BB's 3-bet range.
why_wrong.2: Folding QQ vs a 3-bet is never correct at 100 BB.
why_wrong.3: Shoving QQ folds out JJ, TT which you want calls from.

range_context: UTG raise -> CO 3-bets. AQo from UTG OOP vs CO 3-bet = fold. Dominated by AK in CO's value range.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. CO 3-bets to 9 BB.
question: You have A♦Q♠ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, CO 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: AQo UTG vs CO 3-bet = fold. CO's 3-bet range vs UTG includes AK which dominates AQo. You are OOP for every postflop street. Common leak: calling AQ in 3-bet pots and losing a big pot to AK on an ace-high board. Fold is the correct conservative default.

why_wrong.1: Calling AQo OOP in a 3-bet pot is a common costly leak — dominated by AK regularly.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting AQo into a 3-bet range that includes AK and QQ+ puts you in a terrible spot.
why_wrong.3: Shoving AQo is suicidal.

range_context: UTG raise -> BTN 3-bets. KK 4-bets. QQ optional 4-bet. JJ fold. AKs 4-bets.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. BTN 3-bets to 9 BB.
question: You have 9♣9♦ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, BTN 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: 99 UTG OOP vs BTN 3-bet = fold. Even though BTN 3-bets wider than MP/CO, 99 is still behind JJ+ in a 3-bet pot OOP at 100 BB. The raise/fold line applies: raise with 99 UTG, but fold to a 3-bet. Set-mining in 3-bet pots OOP is not profitable at 100 BB effective.

why_wrong.1: Calling 99 OOP in a 3-bet pot is a leak — you need to hit a set to win, and implied odds don't cover it at 100 BB.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting 99 into a 3-bet range that includes JJ+, AK is suicidal.
why_wrong.3: Shoving 99 is a massive overplay.

range_context: UTG raise -> SB 3-bets. AKs is in the default UTG 4-bet range. Always 4-bet.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. SB 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have A♣K♣ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, SB 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: AKs is in the default UTG 4-bet range. SB's 3-bet range vs UTG includes hands AKs dominates (QQ, JJ, AQ). OOP 4-bet sizing: 2-2.2x the 3-bet = ~22 BB. Data: 4-betting AK yields 797 BB/100 vs 330 BB/100 when calling.

why_wrong.1: Calling AKs OOP in a 3-bet pot is 2.4x less profitable than 4-betting.
why_wrong.2: AKs is ahead of SB's range except AA/KK. Fold is wrong.
why_wrong.3: Shoving folds out hands you want calls from.

range_context: UTG raise -> MP 3-bets. MP 3-bet range vs UTG is very tight. KJs from UTG = fold vs MP 3-bet.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. MP 3-bets to 10.5 BB.
question: You have K♠J♠ in UTG. You raised 3 BB, MP 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: KJs UTG vs MP 3-bet = fold. MP's 3-bet range vs UTG is extremely tight (QQ+, AKs minimum). KJs is dominated by AK, AA, KK and at best flipping with AQ. OOP in a 3-bet pot with a dominated hand = clear fold.

why_wrong.1: Calling KJs OOP in a 3-bet pot vs MP's tight range is a costly leak. You lose big pots vs AK on king-high boards.

question: You have J♠J♦ in MP. UTG raises 3 BB. What do you do?
explanation: JJ is in the cold-call range vs UTG (gap concept hands: JJ-88). UTG's range includes QQ+ which dominates JJ in a 3-bet pot. Call in position — excellent implied odds if you flop a set, fold on A/K/Q boards vs UTG aggression.

why_wrong.1: 3-betting JJ into UTG's tight range builds a large pot where QQ+ dominates you.
why_wrong.2: Folding JJ vs UTG raise is too tight — strong implied odds.
why_wrong.3: Mini-raise is never a valid option.

question: You have 4♠4♦ in MP. UTG raises 3 BB. Effective stacks 100 BB. What do you do?
explanation: 44 is in the MP cold-call range vs UTG as a weaker implied odds hand. 15:1 set-mining rule: calling 3 BB needs 45 BB in effective stacks. 100 BB satisfies this. Call. UTG's strong range means excellent implied odds when you flop a set.

why_wrong.1: 44 vs UTG with 100 BB satisfies the 15:1 rule. Fold is too tight.
why_wrong.2: 3-bet bluffing 44 vs UTG — very low fold equity vs a tight range.
why_wrong.3: Non-standard sizing. Call is correct.

why_wrong.2: 3-bet bluffing 33 vs UTG — very low fold equity.

range_context: MP cold-call vs UTG: AQs-AJs qualify. ATs is in weaker range. But check for aggressive 3-bettor behind.
situation: Conservative ranges. UTG raises 3 BB. Aggressive player on BTN.
question: You have A♣T♣ in MP. UTG raises 3 BB. There is an aggressive 3-bettor on the BTN. What do you do?
options: Always call suited aces in position
explanation: ATs is in the weaker portion of the MP cold-call range vs UTG. But with an aggressive 3-bettor on the BTN, you risk being squeezed into a large pot OOP with a marginal hand. Fold or turn into a 3-bet bluff — do not cold-call with squeeze risk behind you.

why_wrong.1: ATs normally has implied odds but the aggressive BTN changes the calculation entirely.
why_wrong.2: Mini-raise is not valid.
why_wrong.3: Aggressive players behind are a key factor — always consider who is left to act.

why_wrong.1: Calling AA vs a 3-bet misses value — build the pot.
why_wrong.2: Never fold AA.

why_wrong.2: Never fold KK.

explanation: JJ vs a tight CO 3-bet = fold. A tight CO 3-bet range vs MP is QQ+, AKs — JJ is behind QQ+ and flipping with AKs. OOP at 100 BB, fold is correct. The raise/fold line: raise JJ preflop, fold to a tight 3-bet.

why_wrong.1: Calling JJ OOP in a 3-bet pot vs a tight range leads to expensive spots on every A/K/Q board.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting JJ into QQ+/AKs — almost always behind or flipping.

why_wrong.2: AKs is ahead of most 3-bet ranges. Fold is wrong.

question: You have A♣K♦ in MP. You raised 3 BB, BB 3-bets to 10.5 BB. What do you do?
explanation: AKo is listed as optional in the MP 4-bet range. Even as optional, AKo has clear equity advantage vs BB's 3-bet range and should 4-bet. OOP 4-bet: 2-2.2x = ~22 BB. Data: 4-betting AK is 2.4x more profitable than calling.

why_wrong.1: Calling AKo OOP in a 3-bet pot is 2.4x less profitable than 4-betting.
why_wrong.2: AKo is ahead of most 3-bet ranges except AA/KK. Fold is wrong.
why_wrong.3: Shoving folds out hands you want calls from.

range_context: MP 4-bet range: AA, KK, AKs | Optional: QQ, AKo, AQs. 99 and below = fold.
situation: Conservative ranges. You raised 3 BB. CO 3-bets to 9 BB.
question: You have 9♥9♦ in MP. You raised 3 BB, CO 3-bets to 9 BB. What do you do?
explanation: 99 from MP vs CO 3-bet = fold. CO's 3-bet range vs MP includes JJ+, AK at minimum. 99 is behind JJ+ and flipping with AK. OOP at 100 BB, fold. Set-mining implied odds in 3-bet pots do not work at 100 BB effective stacks.

why_wrong.1: Calling 99 OOP in a 3-bet pot — you need to flop a set to win and implied odds don't cover it at 100 BB.
why_wrong.2: 4-betting 99 into JJ+/AK — almost always behind or flipping.
why_wrong.3: Shoving 99 is a massive overplay.
